But you said that would, but, but yeah. Yeah.
So, so essentially the disagreement between us is
that you are using very, very broad language
and then saying, let's see where it lands.
Let's see where it lands. Which is a, I think a,
a legally very uncertain way to do things.
I think you'd need to tighten this up very,
very significantly, which then brings you back into a world
where governments say, we've decided this technology is no
longer going to be around and that one isn't.
Governments can't determine those things. Markets do.
And lenders have to look at where markets are going.
And some of them will say,
no, we'll hang in there for longer.
We think there's some business to be made here.
He's gonna stay in business longer than longer than
that institution thinks.
This is a commercial decision.
You don't need a statute, uh, to start trying
to help make those decisions for them.
Okay. I will leave That one there because
We'll leave that one there and,
and perhaps his worship could take
the, the right honorable Mr.
Upton out for dinner one night in Wellington
and support the Wellton
economy and work through these issues.
Dunno if, I dunno if you agree on much,
but, uh, thank you so much, commissioner for coming in, uh,
via Zoom and thank you members.
Uh, and that concludes, uh, the hearings, uh, uh, for today.
Uh, we'll have some next week.
Thank you to all submitters
and we will, um, uh, we will look forward to going
to the house for question time at two o'clock.
